text: The surgeon uses a right-hand grasper to pull down the stomach, revealing the hiatus hernia. The right crus of the diaphragm is clearly visible adjacent to the hernia.

The surgeon identifies the caudate lobe covered by the pars flaccida of the omentum, with the spleen visible below. Using a harmonic scalpel, the surgeon begins separating the short gastric vessels, creating a small window in the omentum to facilitate further dissection.
The surgeon continues the laparoscopic fundoplication by further separating the short gastric vessels and omentum using a harmonic device, adjusting to minimum mode for larger vessel diameters.

The surgeon carefully carries out the dissection of the omentum, separating all attachments up to the last tip of the gastric fundus. The liver is lifted using Nathanson's liver retractor, providing ergonomic space for the procedure.

The surgeon demonstrates a tension-free wrap technique, ensuring the stomach can move freely. The spleen is clearly visible in the foreground. The surgeon carefully avoids direct contact between the vibrating jaw of the harmonic device and the spleen to prevent bleeding. The dissection is nearly complete at the tip of the stomach.

The surgeon performs anterior peritoneal dissection at the pars flaccida, where the peritoneum appears transparent and devoid of fat deposits. The caudate lobe is visible below, with the right crus also in view. The vena cava lies just beneath the caudate lobe, requiring careful dissection to avoid deep penetration or needle pricking.

The surgeon identifies the right crus and proceeds with anterior mobilization of the esophagus. The stomach is pulled down to create a proper pectoral plane for dissection. In cases of hiatus hernia, the anterior sac formed by the peritoneum is carefully removed using traction techniques to separate it over the crus.
The surgeon controls small oozing by gently applying the vibrating jaw of the harmonic device to the bleeding area.

The surgeon opens the posterior mediastinum, revealing the esophagus for further anterior mobilization. The anterior vagus nerve is visible embedded into the esophagus, requiring careful separation from the posterior vagus nerve. The left crus is identified, and posterior mobilization of the esophagus continues, with proper traction applied. A sling is optional for this procedure, but the surgeon proceeds without using one.

The surgeon performs further posterior mobilization of the esophagus, creating a window by passing the grasper from the right side to the left. This window is enlarged through blunt dissection, facilitating the wrap of the stomach during fundoplication.

The surgeon removes the right extra peritoneum, part of the sac associated with hiatus hernia. A clear window is visible from right to left, through which the stomach will be wrapped from left to right. Care is taken to avoid accidental pulling of the anterior part of the stomach, ensuring a proper shoe shine effect during the wrapping process.

The surgeon performs Cruel Approximation, ensuring the fundus is pulled to create a floppy configuration without tension. The left and right crura are approximated using proline sutures, with a surgeon's knot technique involving a double wrap followed by alternating wraps. A 5mm instrument is used to ensure the approximation is not too tight.

The surgeon performs cruel approximation, carefully taking needle bites from the right crust while avoiding contact with the caudate lobe of the liver and vena cava. Proline suture is used, despite its memory making intracorporeal suturing challenging.

The surgeon completes the crural approximation and proceeds with a Nissen fundoplication, wrapping the stomach around itself. The anterior portion of the stomach is positioned against the right crus, while the posterior portion aligns with the left crus. The wrap measures approximately three to five centimeters, secured with non-absorbable sutures.

The surgeon performs the first wrap of the fundoplication, tightening the surgeon's knot with care to avoid pricking any organs. The cut end of the suture is left slightly longer to prevent it from opening due to the memory of the Proline suture. The second knot is then tied, sandwiching the muscular layer of the esophagus between two layers of the stomach.

The surgeon performs laparoscopic suturing during the fundoplication procedure, taking a third suture bite to secure the wrap around the stomach. The wrap is intended to be between three to five centimeters in length, ensuring it is secure yet floppy.
